vocabulary.garment: a clothing item such as a shirt, pants, or jacket
vocabulary.pattern: 2D, flat, unwrapped version of a garment used to control its overall shape
vocabulary.panel: part of a pattern that makes up the garment
vocabulary.sewing: using thread to stitch two panels together
vocabulary.seams: details created by sewing, can be visible or fold-over seams
vocabulary.folds: drapery folds on a garment or angle between two panels in a sewing line
vocabulary.simulation: computer algorithm used to create the illusion of folds on a garment
vocabulary.arrangement points: Points used for making sure patterns conform to the avatar's surface.
vocabulary.bounding volumes: Volumes based on parts of the avatar (head, torso, arms, legs) that define where arrangement points can be placed.
vocabulary.avatar editor: A section in Marvelous Designer software for editing and adjusting avatar dimensions.
vocabulary.point distance: The distance between each vertex on the mesh, which controls the resolution of folds in Marvellous Designer.
vocabulary.simulation properties: Settings within Marvellous Designer that control aspects like particle distance and shrinkage for fabric simulation.
vocabulary.segment sewing tool: A tool used in Marvelous Designer to sew patterns together.
vocabulary.offset as internal line: A function in Marvelous Designer that creates a visible seam or fold within the garment pattern.
vocabulary.Layer Clone Over: A Marvelous shortcut for cloning a pattern above the original, useful for creating multiple layers in garments.
vocabulary.Layer Clone Under: A Marvelous shortcut for cloning a pattern below the original, useful for creating multiple layers in garments but can cause issues with tight spaces.
vocabulary.linked editing: An automatic linking of cloned patterns that adjusts when the base pattern is modified.
vocabulary.density: number of points used in the simulation affecting garment shape and behavior
vocabulary.normals: facets of a surface that affect how light interacts with it, impacting visualization during editing
vocabulary.grab tool: a tool for selecting and moving parts of an object in 3D modeling software
vocabulary.UV Editor: A tool used to edit the UV coordinates of a model.
vocabulary.simulation 2D Viewport: The view where simulation results are displayed in Marmoset Toolbag.
vocabulary.Outliner: A panel in Marmoset Toolbag that lists and organizes assets in the scene.
vocabulary.Property Editor: A panel in Marmoset Toolbag used to edit properties of selected assets.
vocabulary.Material Editor: A panel in Marmoset Toolbag where materials can be created, edited, and applied to objects.
vocabulary.Reflection tab: A section within the Material Editor that controls reflection settings.
vocabulary.albedo: the base color of a material
vocabulary.diffusion: a setting to control the diffusion of light on a surface, often used for simulating microfiber effects
vocabulary.sheen: a property that adds a glossy or shiny appearance to materials
vocabulary.fresnel: a physical effect that causes more reflection at glancing angles and less at perpendicular ones
vocabulary.volumetric scattering: a technique to simulate light passing through the geometry of an object, often used for skin-like materials
vocabulary.blockout: the initial stage of creating a garment, focusing on the silhouette and relative scale of pieces.
vocabulary.representative state: an intermediate stage in garment creation where the asset matches the concept but is not fully polished.
vocabulary.sternocleidomastoid: a muscle that runs from the lower part of the neck to the sternum and clavicle, often causing discomfort in the neck area.
vocabulary.flat-felled seam: a type of seam where the raw edges are folded over and stitched together, creating a flat edge on the outside of the garment.
vocabulary.twill weave: a weaving pattern that produces a diagonal ribbed texture on the fabric surface.
vocabulary.Pure Ref: A reference viewing app that allows users to paste in images, scale, rotate, flip, add notes, and organize images.
vocabulary.mannequin: A life-size figure used for displaying clothing or as a model for artists.
vocabulary.cosplay: Costuming based on characters from manga, anime, video games, and other media.
vocabulary.snipping tool: A utility that allows users to capture screenshots of specific areas of the screen.
vocabulary.seam lines: Lines on a pattern indicating where fabric edges will be sewn together.
vocabulary.fold angles: Angles at which fabric is folded before cutting to ensure symmetry in the final product.
vocabulary.stitching types: Different methods of stitching used in garment construction, such as flat felled or overlock stitches.
vocabulary.avatar: A digital representation of a character in a game where clothing patterns are applied and simulated.
vocabulary.unfolding with symmetric editing: A technique in Marvelous Designer where fabric is folded before cutting to minimize waste and ensure symmetry.
vocabulary.subdivision: The level of detail in a base mesh model, mentioned as being adjusted for UV mapping purposes.
vocabulary.UV shell mode: A selection method in Maya used to select parts of a model based on UV coordinates.

vocabulary.dampening: A force built into the material properties that resists stretching and buckling.
vocabulary.friction: The resistance between two surfaces, causing parts of the garment to stick while others move.
vocabulary.render scene: A setup within a software application where garments are rendered for visual inspection.
vocabulary.material properties: Characteristics of fabric that determine its behavior, such as elasticity or resistance to folding.
vocabulary.fold appeal: The visual compellingness and artistic interpretation of folds in garments, considering how they wrap around the form, cast shadows, and contribute to the character's narrative.
vocabulary.Renaissance: A period marked by a revival of classical art and philosophy, emphasizing realism and humanism.
vocabulary.Baroque: An artistic style characterized by dynamic movement, intense expression, and contrast, often seen in sculptures and paintings.

tips: Set units to centimeters and view controls to your preferred settings during startup.
Import custom avatars using OBJ files and set load type to 'open' for automatic arrangement points.
Ensure scale is set correctly (100%) when importing models from other software like Maya.
Use arrangement points to ensure patterns align with the avatar's surface.
Automatically generated arrangement points can be manually adjusted or reset to default.
Save custom arrangement points and volumes for future use.
Adjust bounding volumes by modifying length, height, and width settings.
Use XY position in offset to control pattern orientation.
Start with a higher point distance (20) when first sculpting to make it easier to adjust patterns while simulating.
Keep your point distances low until you're ready to move on to the next stage of design.
Use the GPU processor for real-time adjustments in shrinkage settings to get better previews of final folds.
Duplicate existing patterns and use them as a base for new designs, adjusting particle distance and other properties as needed.
Adjust the arm size of sleeves and top portion to match the body size (around 45 centimeters).
Reduce sleeve length by pulling down from the top and up from the bottom.
Remove taper if the jacket has thick sleeves.
Simulate garment after making changes to see how it looks in real-time.
Use the Rectangle tool to create elastic portions of the sleeve.
Clone Symmetric Pattern Sewing for creating symmetrical patterns like the cuff.
Set shrinkage weft to 80 or lower to simulate fabric stretch and fit around the wrist.
Adjust fold angles on collar, zipper stand, and internal lines to improve garment fit.
Ensure edge seams match up almost perfectly between front and back panels for consistent internal lines.
Use Layer Clone Over to create multiple layers without causing issues in tight spaces.
Remove linked editing on layer clones and base patterns before making adjustments.
Flip normals on interior patterns to ensure they face the opposite direction of the layer clone.
Adjust pressure settings carefully to avoid garments flying away or collapsing during simulation.
Experiment with different shrinkage values and particle distances to achieve desired fabric behavior.
Adjust elements like hoods and zippers iteratively to get the final look before wrapping up.
Simulate garments frequently to see how they behave under different conditions.
Ensure thin panels are properly weighted during simulation to avoid folding issues.
Right click in the empty space of the UV Editor and select Reset UV to 2D Arrangement to align all UVs with their corresponding patterns.
Use Fit All UV to 0 to 1 to shrink and move all UVs into the primary UV space (0 to 1).
Create a silhouette shader by adding a new material, setting its color to black, and renaming it Avatar.
Set the Diffusion Type to Unlit in the Material Editor to remove any lighting information from the avatar's default material.
Import your avatar using File > Import or by dragging it into the scene.
To export garments, select all shirt panels, unfreeze them, navigate to File > Export > OBJ Selected, and choose where to save the file.
Export as a single object if you want all selected panels joined together in one 3D object; export as multiple objects if each panel should be its own object.
Adjust the exposure of the environment map for better lighting control (e.g., reduce it to around 0.5).
Use an Abandoned House preset sky for reflections, and adjust the background color and blur settings to add interest to the render.
Create a 3-point light setup: key light, fill light, and rim light.
Position the key light to create dramatic shadows across the garment (e.g., parallel to the side of the jacket).
Duplicate the key light for the fill light, rotate it to the angle of the shadows, and move it to the left side of the body to soften the shadows.
Add a rim light behind the avatar, angled down slightly, with higher brightness and wider cone angle to accentuate the silhouette.
Set the color to mid-gray when working with cloth shader simulation.
Use microfiber settings in the diffusion property to fake a cloth shader.
Adjust sheen and its roughness to create different effects on the material surface.
Use transmission to simulate a cloth BRDF (Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function).
Set the transmission to volumetric scattering, adjust scatter depth for desired softness or washout effect.
Add detail normals to fake tiling patterns of the cloth. Adjust tile amount and offset for better results.
Change reflection mode from GGX to anisotropic for creating a more line-like highlight on materials like nylon jackets.
Adjust anisotropy, glossiness, and roughness for desired effects in anisotropic reflections.
Use contrast between multiple garments by varying color, value, roughness, glossiness, specularity, isotropic, and GGX properties.
Edit camera field of view to flatten the image and make more of the jacket visible.
Enable Depth of Field with appropriate near blur and focus distance settings.
Adjust tone mapping and sky brightness for desired lighting effects.
Use bloom to add a glow effect to the render.
Enable ambient occlusion to add lighting variation in folds where textures are not present.
Balance the strength and size of ambient occlusion for subtle results.
Turn on local reflections to simulate secondary raycasts from global illumination.
Adjust light colors to match the material being rendered, e.g., making shadows slightly more orange due to bounced light.
Use Toolbag or Unreal for rendering with PNG or JPEG files at 2K or 4K resolution.
Create a simple 3-point light setup and use advanced material properties in Toolbag for decent renders.
Import the base mesh of the bandit mask slash collar to offset the outside line for better fold control.
Use a custom preset that balances stiffness when creating garments with complex materials like leather.
spend time analyzing the concept before diving into modeling
break down the concept to identify and name different pieces of the outfit
research by naming specific elements in the design (e.g., sternocleidomastoid, flat-felled seam)
look up reference materials based on named elements
Use Pure Ref for organizing reference images with features like scaling, rotating, flipping, adding notes, and image organization.
Utilize a combination of Pinterest and Google image searches for finding references.
Look for garment references on actors or models to get a better understanding of human poses and volume.
Be cautious of cheap costumes found in places like Halloween stores as they may not be accurate.
Use the snipping tool in Pure Ref by pressing Windows Key Shift S on Windows 10 or searching for it via the start button.
Check websites hosting images to find additional angles and close-up shots that can aid in marketing.
Use free online resources or purchase detailed reference patterns for creating believable clothing in games.
Understand the function of different sections on a pattern, such as interior, exterior, and padding materials.
Label each panel with letters corresponding to stitching instructions and numbers indicating how many pieces need to be cut.
Fold fabric along lines marked with arrows before cutting to ensure symmetry (e.g., for C panels).
Consider the grain direction of fabrics when cutting patterns; typically vertical but sometimes bias-cutting is necessary.
Keep point distance above 10 for visible folds but not too detailed.
Use models from another package as a base and adjust them with UV shells and duplicate faces tool.
Ensure UV's are in 0 to 1 space before exporting.
Maintain at least 16 pixels between UV islands to avoid confusion by the algorithm.
Start by duplicating your base mesh to use as a starting point for garments in ZBrush.
Use deformation tab tools like inflation to sculpt forms closer to the desired fit.
Mask off regions for specific parts of the garment (e.g., sleeves, side seams) and create polygroups for UVs.
Smooth out edges using polish by groups mode before zero meshing at a higher target polygon count to maintain detail.
Use history markers in ZBrush to save geometry states for later reference or projection purposes.
Utilize the fitting suit feature in Marvelous to auto-fit garments from the library assets to your avatar's measurements.
Import and dissect library assets to learn how different garments are made.
Use music or background tasks while working to increase efficiency.
Measure out the base mesh using heads for consistency, then scale the effect as needed.
Extract portions of the mesh and use them as starting points for blocking out garments.
Use Multi-Cut tool for creating folds like lapel lines.
Split models in back and mirror across X-axis to create symmetry.
Dissect different patterns from clothing DIY websites to understand collar structures better.
Swap between brushes and moving individual vertices for more control over garment modifications.
Use Smooth Preview to get an idea of the final look with lower point distance.
Split garments into their own materials when importing colors from OBJ files.
Bounce around UVing and modeling in stages to avoid getting fixated on one part.
Place buttons on the bottom surface and buttonholes on the top for correct placement.
Decrease point distance slightly to give more points to move around for better fit adjustments.
Adjust gravity settings for different fold effects (default is 9.8 meters per second).
Use sewing technique to control tension and create specific folds.
Manipulate damping values to reduce or increase tension in the fabric.
Understand how friction affects the garment's movement and folding behavior.
Use example files provided as a guide for how far along your project should be.
Think about the lighting and camera angle early in the process.
Name each submitted item using a consistent naming convention: last name, underscore, week number.
Consider how folds can enhance the narrative of a character by being either baggy and unkempt or pristine and sharp.
Use lighting to create pooling shadows and glaring highlights that draw attention to specific areas of the garment.
Incorporate dynamic composition and movement in your designs, as seen in Baroque period sculptures.
Use the Line 3D Pattern Tool to draw out 3D shapes for garments like pockets or armor panels.
Select and edit 3D pattern lines using the Edit Line 3D Pattern Tool.
Convert selected 3D pattern lines to internal lines for sewing purposes.
